engineering.translation: Krishna distinguishes two stages of the practitioner. For the engineer climbing toward yoga — the muni still ascending — action is the means of ascent. For the same engineer once established, calm is the means by which higher practice proceeds. The verse is structural: it explains why the chapter will move shortly from action-instruction to meditation-instruction. The aspirant in year three of the practice does not have available the same operative means as the senior in year fifteen. For the year-three engineer the work itself — the migration, the deploy, the on-call shift — is what produces the discipline. For the year-fifteen engineer the active work has done what it can do; from that point onward calm — śama, the cessation of agitation — is what advances the practice further. Shankara reads the staging strictly; Ramanuja reads it as a continuum. Both agree that the means is staged.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A senior tech lead at a SaaS company watches his team's two newest engineers, Jordan in their first year and Priya in their fifth. He gives them different advice and notices the pattern: with Jordan he is constantly assigning work — pick up the migration ticket, take the on-call shift, lead the post-mortem, draft the design doc for the Postgres-vector-store experiment. The work is what is making Jordan into an engineer. With Priya the advice has changed — she has done the migrations and the on-call shifts, twice each. Now what advances her practice is something different: the discipline of not picking up the next ticket the moment the prior one resolves; the discipline of holding a Datadog graph open for ten minutes without closing it; the discipline of saying nothing in standup when she has nothing useful to add. For Priya the means has shifted from action to śama. The TL recognises 6.3's structural claim in his own muscle-memory: he gives different advice to the two engineers because they are at different stages on the same staircase.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader uses it to claim that senior engineers should stop merging code or running on-call. The verse does not enjoin cessation of action for the established practitioner; it names śama (calm) as the additional operative means at that stage. The senior continues to act; what changes is what produces further ascent.
engineering.counter_example: When a year-one engineer reads this verse and concludes they should pursue śama directly — sit in their workspace doing nothing, refuse the migration ticket — they have inverted the staging. The verse is explicit: for the ascending muni, action is the means. The śama of 6.3 is not available as a starting move; it is the means after the action-stage has done its work.
engineering.implication: Locate yourself on the staircase honestly. If the work itself is still producing the discipline, do not skip ahead to śama. If the active work has done what it can do, do not refuse the calm that the next stage requires. The means is staged.
engineering.quotable_line: For the engineer ascending, action is the means; for the one ascended, calm is the means — the staging is not optional.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 6.3 engineering layer drafted from triangulated source pack with anchoring to chapters/06-thesis.md. Chapter 6 — staged-practitioner doctrine; concrete TL/IC pairing.
